coredata.dc:title: A waste-free entropy-driven DNA nanomachine for smartly designed photoelectrochemical biosensing of MicroRNA-155
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yuhang
Xu, Lingqiu
Xu, Qin
Wu, Yuqin
Li, Jing
Li, Hongbo
coredata.dc:description: DNA nanotechnology has been booming in many fields such as biosensors, logic gates, and material science. Typically, as a kind of powerful isothermal and enzyme-free DNA amplifier in biosensors, entropy-driven DNA nanomachines are superior to hairpin-based ones in speed, specificity, stability, and simplicity. However, the atomic economy of non-covalent molecular reactions in these machines is not high, and DNAs waste is typically generated during operation. Herein, in order to further save costs and improve the performance, we report a novel design for a smart photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor of microRNA-155 by engineering waste-free entropy-driven DNA amplifiers conjugated to superparamagnetic Fe3O4@SiO2 particles. This elegant design efficiently avoids leaving redundant DNA strands and waste complex in the amplification system, and all the displaced DNA strands can be regenerated into double-stranded structures, making the reaction irreversible. Thanks to superparamagnetic Fe3O4@SiO2 particles, this strategy is achieved by effectively enriching, extracting, and cleaning target analogs to prevent co-existing species from remaining on the modified electrode surface, enabling a highly specific and sensitive PEC biosensor. This innovative study will be a new perspective on microRNAs detection in complex biological systems, paving the way for the design of waste-free DNA molecular machines and promoting the development of DNA nanotechnology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemistry
Superparamagnetism
Biosensor
Entropy-driven DNA amplifier
MicroRNA-155

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM images of Fe3O4@SiO2 particles, (B) CdSe QDs, and (C) enlarged drawing of Figure B; Elemental mappings of Fe3O4@SiO2 (D), Fe (E), O (F), and Si (G).
The XPS spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2 particles (A), Fe 2p (B), O 1s (C), and Si 2p (D).
Chain displacement reaction equation with thermodynamic parameters.

(A) Electrophoretic characterization for different samples. Lane 1: 2 μmol L−1 × 5 μL DNA strand L; Lane 2: a mixture of 2 μmol L−1 × 5 μL DNA strands L, R, and D; Lane 3: Lane 2 + target miRNA-155 (1 μmol L−1 × 5 μL); Lane 4: 2 μmol L−1 × 5 μL DNA strand L, R, D, F + target miRNA-155 (1 μmol L−1 × 0.5 μL). (B, C) The electrochemical impedance spectra of glassy carbon electrodes modified with (a) CdSe QDs, (b) CdSe QDs + DNA strand D, (c) Fe3O4@SiO2, (d) Fe3O4@SiO2 + DNA strand L, and (e) CdSe QDs-DNA strand D-DNA strand R-DNA strand L-Fe3O4@SiO2 particles in 2.5 mmol L−1 K4Fe(CN)6 and K3Fe(CN)6 solution.

(A) The photocurrents of (a) the composite (Ⅰ) and partial structure (II) (b) modified ITO electrodes measured in 0.1 mol L−1 PBS (pH 7.0) in the absence (a) and the presence (b) of 5 pmol L−1 miRNA-155. (B) Specificity investigation for target miRNA-155 (1 fmol L−1) over co-existing miRNA-122 (500 fmol L−1), miRNA-196a (500 fmol L−1), miRNA-20b (500 fmol L−1), miRNA-10b (500 fmol L−1), and the complex (each 200 fmol L−1). (C) The photoelectric signals measured in 0.1 mol L−1 PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0, 10, 50, 100, 500 amol L−1, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500 fmol L−1, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100 pmol L−1 miRNA-155. (D) The corresponding calibration curve.
Schematic illustration of the construction of the PEC sensor for miRNA-155.

head.ce:title: A waste-free entropy-driven DNA nanomachine for smartly designed photoelectrochemical biosensing of MicroRNA-155

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng, 224051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, 225002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: DNA nanotechnology has been booming in many fields such as biosensors, logic gates, and material science. Typically, as a kind of powerful isothermal and enzyme-free DNA amplifier in biosensors, entropy-driven DNA nanomachines are superior to hairpin-based ones in speed, specificity, stability, and simplicity. However, the atomic economy of non-covalent molecular reactions in these machines is not high, and DNAs waste is typically generated during operation. Herein, in order to further save costs and improve the performance, we report a novel design for a smart photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor of microRNA-155 by engineering waste-free entropy-driven DNA amplifiers conjugated to superparamagnetic Fe3O4@SiO2 particles. This elegant design efficiently avoids leaving redundant DNA strands and waste complex in the amplification system, and all the displaced DNA strands can be regenerated into double-stranded structures, making the reaction irreversible. Thanks to superparamagnetic Fe3O4@SiO2 particles, this strategy is achieved by effectively enriching, extracting, and cleaning target analogs to prevent co-existing species from remaining on the modified electrode surface, enabling a highly specific and sensitive PEC biosensor. This innovative study will be a new perspective on microRNAs detection in complex biological systems, paving the way for the design of waste-free DNA molecular machines and promoting the development of DNA nanotechnology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Growing research suggests that microRNAs (miRNAs) have become markers of cancer (Simmel et al., 2019). Specific diseases can be identified by the profiles of miRNA expression (Peng et al., 2020). Therefore, the high-performance monitoring of miRNAs plays a pivotal role in the diagnosis and treatment of diseases (Liang et al., 2017). However, miRNA in real samples is characterized by small size, low expression level, similar sequence as well as wide concentration range, these pose a great challenge to the detection (Shi et al., 2018). Usually, introducing signal amplification is a path to solve this difficult problem. Based on the mechanism of releasing free energy when a new base pair is formed, catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) (Cheng et al., 2019; Karunanayake Mudiyanselage et al., 2018; Simmel et al., 2019) and hybridization chain reaction (HCR) (Li et al., 2020a; Wang et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2020) amplification strategies have been proposed. Also, hairpin-free and entropy-driven DNA amplifiers provide an effective solution to the discovery of low-expression biomarkers (Ma et al., 2019a; Meng et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2018). The pioneering work was introduced by Zhang and coworkers in 2007 (Zhang et al., 2007). This exclusive strategy is driven by the unique entropy-driven force with substantial advantages of simplicity, high-speed, and stability (Gao et al., 2021; Lv et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2021). In addition, in such an entropy increase system, the base pairs that remain unchanged before and after the reaction reduce the possibility of circuit leakage and improve reliability (Liang et al., 2017). Since then, this strategy is becoming increasingly popular for determination of low-abundance miRNAs in cells. For instance, Tan group have discussed the mechanisms and merits of entropy-driven DNA machines, and even proposed an entropy beacon (abbreviated as Ebeacon) for a highly sensitive and specific biosensor (Lv et al., 2015). Recently, by conjugating Ebeacon to superparamagnetic nanostructures, we have developed two photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensors for miRNAs detection (Li et al. 2020c, 2021b). Although these Ebeacon-based biosensors have great performance in sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and accuracy, waste DNA strands are inevitably released during the operation of these entropy-driven DNA amplifiers. From the perspective of improving the atomic economy of non-covalent molecular reactions, it is a pursuit to build an entropy-driven DNA amplifier without waste strands.

To achieve highly sensitive and specific bioassays, it is highly essential to extract the targets from complex real samples in a nondestructive manner (Ling et al., 2022; Roth et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2021). Superparamagnetic nanocrystals are alternative to effectively isolate the coexisting species before bioanalysis because their dispersion is not affected by strong magnetic interactions (Deng et al., 2008; Ge et al., 2007b). Fe3O4 nanocrystals have attracted most attention in response to this goal because of their excellent biocompatibility and stability (Ge et al., 2007a). Recently, magnetic separation strategy and various biological signal amplification technologies have been introduced to PEC bioanalysis (Fu et al., 2022; Lv et al., 2018; Zhang et al. 2010, 2018). Because of its high sensitivity, low noise background, as well as cost-effectiveness and ease of operation, PEC sensing has stimulated the interest of more researchers (Fu et al., 2020; Ge et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2019b; Wang et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2020). However, those designs are all based on the introduction of functionalized magnetic beads for separation and inclined to utilize the signal indicator element left in the liquid phase for further fabrication. Obviously, the coexisting species in complex samples can still remain on the photoelectrodes, resulting in a limited selectivity. Therefore, after biorecognition and biological signal amplification, extraction from real complex samples and in situ cleaning of signal indicators is an alternative to further development of highly specific PEC biosensors (Li et al., 2020b).

In this work, based on a waste-free entropy-driven biomagnification strategy coupled with superparamagnetic Fe3O4@SiO2 particles, a novel PEC biosensor with miRNA-155 as the target model was successfully developed (Scheme 1 ). First, single stranded DNA (ssDNA) R and ssDNA D labelled with CdSe QDs could be partially hybridized with ssDNA L-Fe3O4@SiO2. Second, the formed double strand DNA (dsDNA) composite I (R & D: L) could be triggered by the target miRNA-155, after sequential strand displacement with the help of fuel ssDNA F, a new dsDNA composite II (F: L) formed, while target T will be displaced and initiate the next cycle. Also, strand R, ssDNA D labelled with CdSe QDs, and ssDNA L-Fe3O4@SiO2 will re-assemble the composite I. Obviously, once introduction of composite I, with the trigger of a target, there will be many copies of composite II coming into being until the fuel was exhausted irrespective of the reaction time. In a limited reaction time, the more targets existed, the more composite II formed. Under the action of a unique and exclusive entropy-driven force, after multiple cycle amplifications, low-abundance miRNA-155 spontaneously induces the formation of a large number of structures (II) and obtains a proportional decrease in photocurrent by releasing strand D connected with CdSe quantum dots (QDs). Compared to the striking photocurrent response of CdSe QDs in composite I, there is nearly no photocurrent response left when converting to composite II. Thus, it is concluded that there is a quantitative linear relationship between the decrease in photocurrent and the increased concentration of miRNA-155 in a certain range. This strategy really removes the coexisting species from modified electrodes by efficient in situ magnetic extraction and cleaning, leading to highly specific PEC bioassays. In addition, without the utilization of enzymes, the proposed PEC sensor has the strengths of low cost, good stability, strong reproducibility, high analytical efficiency, and easy handling. In particular, it is worth mentioning that no waste is left in the operation of this DNA nanomachine. This innovative study would serve as a novel perspective for PEC bioanalysis.

Firstly, the activating reagent of EDC/NHS (400 μL, 0.1 mol L−1) was mixed with 1.5 mL of DNA buffer solution for 5 min, then CdSe QDs (1 mL) was dispersed in above solution and reacted for 3 h at 25 °C followed by addition of strand D (100 μL, 2 μmol L−1) and reacted for 4 h. Then strand D bound to CdSe QDs were successfully prepared and collected by centrifugation. Simultaneously, strand L (100 μL, 2 μmol L−1) was activated for 3 h at 25 °C by EDC/NHS (400 μL, 0.1 mol L−1) in DNA buffer solution (1.5 mL) followed by addition of Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2 suspension (1 mL) and reacted for 4 h. And the resulting Fe3O4@SiO2 linked with DNA strand L were collected by magnetic separation and washed for three times. Afterward, CdSe QDs-strand D and Fe3O4@SiO2-strand L were scattered into DNA buffer solution (2 mL) and incubated with strand R (100 μL, 2 μmol L−1) at 4 °C for 12 h. After magnetic extraction, the resulting structure (I) was scattered into DNA buffer solution (1 mL) followed by addition of strand T at different concentrations, and incubated for 3 h at 25 °C. As a result, the detached strand R was recovered for the formation of structure (I). Next, the generated structure M2 reacted with 100 μL of 2 μmol L−1 strand F for 3 h, and the resulting structure (II) was extracted by an external magnetic field. Then, the detached strand T was reused for the next cycle of this entropy-driven DNA circuit bioamplification system. Meanwhile, the detached strand D was reused to re-assemble the structure (I). Subsequently, 20 μL of the same concentration of structure (I) and (II) were severally used to prepare the modified electrodes by coating them on cleaned ITO electrodes and drying them at 25 °C. Before the photocurrent detection, the magnetic field has been removed. The three-electrode system was chosen, with the chopped light irradiation, in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.0), the current-time curves were recorded at a bias voltage of 0 V and the calibration curve was plotted. For practical applications, the analysis steps are the same as those described above by replacing the miRNA-155 standard solution with serum samples.

The microstructures and morphologies of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, and CdSe QDs were imaged by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Fig. S1 shows that the average diameter of Fe3O4 nanocrystals is approximately 92 nm. In order to prevent Fe3O4 nanocrystals from spreading out and further improve the functionality of its surface, SiO2 coating was performed. And it can be seen from Fig. 1 A that the Fe3O4@SiO2 particles with a diameter size of about 172 nm have been obtained. Further, the element mapping was used to characterize the material morphological structure and element distribution, as can be seen from Fig. 1D–G, a well-defined Fe3O4@SiO2 core-shell structure is obtained, and the corresponding element distribution is shown. In addition, Fig. 1B shows that the mean size of CdSe QDs is approximately 3.4 nm. Also, the lattice fringes of the particles were magnified (Fig. 1C) and the lattice spacing is determined to be 0.339 nm, which corresponds to the orientation of the CdSe (111) plane (Liu et al., 2013), indicating that the crystalline CdSe QDs have been successfully synthesized.

To further verify the successful synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2 particles, they were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 A shows the XPS spectrum of element Fe 2p, O 1s, and Si 2p, which can be ascribed to Fe3O4 and SiO2 species. Specifically, the two peaks of 710.6 eV (Fe 2p3/2) and 723.8 eV (Fe 2p1/2) of Fe 2p orbital electrons in Fig. 2B are attributed to trivalent and divalent Fe, respectively. The O 1s with a peak of 530.4 eV in Fig. 2C can be attributed to the oxygen element in Fe3O4 (Li et al., 2018). In Fig. 2C–D, Si 2p with a peak of 103.2 eV and O 1s with a peak of 532.5 eV can be ascribed to SiO2 (Zhou et al., 2009). These indicate that the core-shell Fe3O4@SiO2 particles were achieved. It is well consistent with the element mappings and XRD characterizations (Li et al., 2020b). Also, the superparamagnetic features of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@SiO2 at room temperature have been confirmed in the previous work (Li et al., 2021a).

As shown in Scheme 1, DNA ternary composite structure (I) was formed by superparamagnetic Fe3O4@SiO2 combined with DNA strand L, CdSe QDs linked strand D, and strand R on the base of complementary base pairing rules. In this state, the 4* site on L is exposed as a toehold domain, then target T bound to the toehold domain 4* on L to generate the four-chain transition structure. It then rearranged, resulting in the generation of M1. Since the binding force between 2 and 2* is not enough, the binding between R and L is unstable, resulting in M1 spontaneously generating M2 and R, which can be reused by magnetic separation. As R is detached, the newly exposed toehold domain 2* then promotes the binding of F to M2, thereby producing M3, which is then rapidly rearranged to release strand D and beacon (II). The released D and R can be used to rebuild DNA ternary composite structure (I) for next cycle. Driven by the unique and exclusive entropy-driven force, even the low-abundance miRNA-155 could induce a large number of structure (II) formation after many cycles. Compared to the photocurrent resulted from structure (I) modified ITO electrode, the remaining structure (I) and structure (II)-based photocurrent proportionally decreases with the increase concentration of miRNA-155.

To prove that it is an entropy-driven DNA amplifier, the corresponding thermodynamic theory has been introduced to calculate the change of Gibbs free energy (Fig. 3 ). Calculated via free NUPACK software, ΔG of −3.28 kcal/mol was obtained. According to equation (1), where ΔH ≈ 0, since the amounts of base pairs keep unchanged before and after amplification. Therefore, TΔS acts as a driving force for the amplifier.
(1)
Δ G
=
Δ H
−
T Δ S
Further calculation result indicates that conversion of reaction system is greater than 99.999%. The specific calculation process can be found in supporting information.

To further confirm the operation of the bioamplification system, polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) was introduced to characterize the strand displacement reaction. The detailed characterization approach referred to the previous work (Li et al., 2021a). As shown in Fig. 4 A, lanes 1–4 correspond to four samples as follows: 2 μmol L−1 × 5 μL DNA strand L; 2 μmol L−1 × 5 μL DNA strand L, R and D; 2 μmol L−1 × 5 μL DNA strand L, R, D and 1 μmol L−1 × 5 μL target strand miRNA 155; 2 μmol L−1 × 5 μL DNA strand L, R, D, F, and 1 μmol L−1 × 0.5 μL target strand miRNA 155. Lane 1 shows the band of the single-strand DNA L, and then the three DNA strands L, R, and D formed a DNA ternary composite B as a substrate for the entire entropy-driven system (Lane 2). When 1 μmol L−1 × 5 μL target miRNA-155 was added, the single-strand R was shed and a complex intermediate was produced (Lane 3). Finally, the complex intermediate reacted with F, releasing target and D (Lane 4). Even if 5% target miRNA-155 could exhaust the whole DNA ternary composite B. Here, the target did not show in lane 4 since it was involved in the next cycle reaction. The bright fringes in Fig. 4A can be assigned to various DNA chain structures according to their different molecular weights, further suggesting that a completely entropy-driven process successfully operates the reaction system.

To prove the DNA strands successfully assembled on the nanomaterials, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed (Fig. 4B and C). Initially, both Fe3O4@SiO2 and CdSe QDs particles modified glassy carbon electrodes exhibit small semicircle diameter in the Nyquist plots (curves a, c). When they were separately bonded with DNA strands by the activation of EDC/NHS, the corresponding charge-transfer resistance (Rct) values were increased (curves b, d). It suggests that the anchored DNA strands efficiently hinder the electron transfer from the nanomaterials to the electrodes. This phenomenon illustrates the successful modification of DNA strands. Compared to curve b and curve d, the Rct of curve e increases to 6300 Ω, indicating that the DNA amplifiers labelled with CdSe QDs have been successfully conjugated to Fe3O4@SiO2 particles.

To further evaluate the DNAs conjugations and the content of DNAs on CdSe QDs and Fe3O4@SiO2 particles, FT-IR and UV–vis spectra were chosen to characterize (Figs. S2 and S3). The results indicate that the strand DNA L and strand DNA D have been confirmed to conjugate on Fe3O4@SiO2 particles and CdSe QDs, respectively. And the content of DNAs captured on Fe3O4@SiO2 particles and CdSe QDs is 70.5% and 78.6%, respectively.

Based on the modified electrodes, photocurrent measurements were also utilized to evaluate the bioamplification strategy. As shown in Fig. 5 A, without a target, the ternary DNA complex structure (I)-based photocurrent is of 125.2 nA (curve a). When initiated by target miRNA-155 (5 pmol L−1) and with the assistance of the fuel (DNA strand F), the DNA strands D linked with CdSe QDs were detached from structure (I). Driven by the increased entropy, the superparamagnetic composites containing structure (II) were separated and assembled on an ITO electrode, and then the photoelectric response of 82.1 nA was obtained (curve b). It can be concluded that the photocurrent decrement can be used to monitor the low-abundance miRNA-155.

Based on the as-designed enzyme- and waste-free PEC sensor, different concentrations of miRNA-155 solution were utilized to initiate the entropy-driven signal amplification system, the superparamagnetic complex containing structure (II) was then assembled onto the electrode and the photoelectric response was measured separately. Compared to the photocurrent of the structure (I)-based photoanode, the gradually decreased photoelectric signals were obtained with the concentration of miRNA-155 increase (Fig. 5C). And a dynamic linear range (10 amol L−1-10 pmol L−1) was achieved. Also, the detection limit of 3.2 amol L−1 was worked out by 3σ/k. Here, σ termed as standard deviation was calculated by the background signal (5 times), and k is the slope of the calibration curve (Fig. 5D). The detection limit is lower than others that have been reported (Table S2). The ultrasensitive detection is mainly attributed to the efficient operation of entropy-driven biological amplifier and magnetic enrichment. The wide linear range (six orders of magnitude) is caused by the high-density assembly of superparamagnetic composites containing structure (II) on ITO electrodes. In Table S2, the other methods and detection time can be listed as follows: fluorescence (2 h, Zhang et al., 2015a; 2.5 h, Miao et al., 2018), SERS (1 h, He et al., 2017), ECL (18 h, Liang et al., 2022; 1 h, Zhou et al., 2018; 6 h, Zhang et al., 2015b), electrochemistry (7 h, Liang et al., 2019), and PEC (7.5 h, Yang et al., 2022). In this work, the entire testing process took 7 h to complete, during which time the entropy-driven DNA amplification consumed 6 h at 25 °C, while the PEC response collection only needed 30 s. Therefore, the unique entropy-driven DNA amplification process takes up the vast majority of the detection time, it is different from other signal amplification strategies, let alone for those strategies without any biosignal amplification. Generally speaking, the detection efficiency of the as-designed PEC biosensor is acceptable. Moreover, the PEC sensor has additional strengths of zero waste, low cost, and easy manipulation.

Stability as a key parameter of PEC sensors has been estimated. Fig. 5A shows only a slight change in the photoelectric signal after 4 times chopped illumination, indicating the stability of the PEC sensor. After one month, the PEC sensor still exhibited 95.7% of the initial photocurrent (Fig. S4), suggesting that the as-designed PEC sensor can be stored at 4 °C for a longer time. Also, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.62% can be obtained from the calibration curve of five freshly manufactured PEC sensors, suggesting that the PEC sensor can be reproduced.

Also, the specificity has been investigated. As shown in Fig. 5B, compared to the photoelectric signal initiated by 1 fmol L−1 miRNA-155, the photocurrents were all less than 5% even when we added the coexisting substances such as miRNA-122 (500-fold), miRNA-196a (500-fold), miRNA-20b (500-fold), miRNA-10b (500-fold), and the complex (each 200-fold), indicating good selectivity of the proposed PEC biosensor. It benefits from DNA specific identification and magnetic separation technique to effectively extract targets from complex systems and to measure photocurrents in pure PBS.

Generally speaking, a signal-off sensor is really liable to be influenced by its degradation. In this protocol, however, the unique design of only one-step electrode modification efficiently reduces the sensor degradation, different from the layer-by-layer assembly design and multiple washing steps for conventional electrode modification. Also, the achieved photocurrent only comes from the sole photoelectric beacon of CdSe QDs in the composite Ⅰ, which is beneficial for the stability of the modified electrode in contrast to that of single CdSe QDs. These enable the reliability of the PEC biosensor.

To evaluate the practical application of the as-designed PEC biosensing system, healthy human serum diluted 20 times was selected as real samples and measured with a standard addition method. Table S3 shows that the recoveries of miRNA-155 are between 94.50% and 103.70% for the suggested spiked samples. Such high recoveries with a low RSD of 4.33% indicate the as-fabricated PEC biosensor is reliable.

Briefly, a waste-free entropy-driven DNA nanomachine was designed for construction of a robust PEC biosensor of miRNA-155. The driving force for this biosignal amplification comes from entropy, not the free energy released by the formation of new base pairs. Also, the double-stranded assembly substrate makes the sequence design more flexible and stable compared to the structure of the DNA hairpins. In this entropy increase system, the base pairs before and after the reaction remain unchanged and exhibit strong thermal stability, avoiding interference from other coexistences. Particularly, this amazing design effectively avoids redundant DNA strands and waste compounds left in the recycling system, and all displaced DNA strands can be used to regenerate the double-stranded structure to make the reaction irreversible. Besides, this strategy enables highly specific PEC bioassay through efficient magnetic extraction and cleaning to truly remove co-existing species from modified electrodes. The ultra-sensitivity mostly benefits from the unique and exclusive entropy-driven DNA amplifier and magnetic enrichment. In addition, the PEC sensor is low-cost, stable, repeatable, easy operating, and time saving. This innovative study will chart a new path for efficient PEC detection in complex biological systems.

Yuhang Chen: Development or design of methodology; creation of waste-free entropy-driven DNA nanomachine; experimental work. Lingqiu Xu: Ideas; formulation or evolution of overarching research goals and aims. Qin Xu: NUPACK software; Calculation of entropy increase. Yuqin Wu: Provision of study materials and reagents; Partial experimental work. Jing Li: Specifically critical review, commentary or revision-including pre-or postpublication stages. Hongbo Li: Oversight and leadership responsibility for the research activity planning and execution, including mentorship external to the core team; Acquisition of the financial support for the project leading to this publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (22176165, 22076161, 21775135), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211362, BK20201473), Yangzhou University Interdisciplinary Research Foundation for Chemistry Discipline of Targeted Support (yzuxk202009), and this work was also sponsored by Qinglan Project of Jiangsu Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supporting Information: The reagents, apparatus, synthesis of Fe3O4 colloidal nanocrystal clusters, Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2, and CdSe QDs, and partial characterizations were described in Supporting Information.

Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2022.114569.
